shortDescription: Surgery that creates or refines an upper-eyelid crease to make the eyes look larger and more open.

sections.overview:
Double eyelid surgery creates a defined crease in the upper eyelid for people born without one, or refines an existing crease to make the eyes appear larger and more rested. Patients travel to Korea because the procedure is one of the most frequently performed cosmetic operations there, giving surgeons extensive experience in tailoring the crease height and shape to suit each face naturally rather than applying one fixed look.

The goal is a crease that fits your eye anatomy and proportions. Some patients want a subtle, low crease that simply opens the eye slightly; others prefer a more defined fold. Many people from regions where a natural crease is uncommon choose this surgery to reduce the heaviness of the upper lid and make applying makeup or simply looking awake easier.

There are two main techniques. The non-incisional, or buried suture, method passes fine sutures through small punctures to anchor the crease without a long cut, suiting patients with thinner lids and little excess skin. The incisional method uses a fine cut along the planned crease to remove a small amount of skin or fat and create a more durable, defined fold, suiting heavier or more variable lids.

An epicanthoplasty, which adjusts the small fold at the inner corner of the eye, is sometimes added to lengthen the eye's appearance, but it is a separate decision. This surgery is distinct from ptosis correction, which addresses a drooping lid caused by weak muscle and changes how much the eye opens, not just the crease. The stated stay of about 5 days allows for consultation, surgery, and early healing before you fly home.
sections.candidacy:
Good candidates are healthy adults who want a more defined or symmetric eyelid crease, who understand the difference between a cosmetic crease and a functional lid problem, and who have realistic expectations about a natural-looking change. Patients with thin lids and minimal excess skin are often suited to the non-incisional method, while those with heavier lids, excess skin, or fat may benefit more from the incisional approach.

The surgery is not advisable during active eye infection, for people with uncontrolled dry-eye disease, bleeding disorders, or unstable chronic illness. If your main concern is a lid that droops and partly covers the pupil, that may be ptosis, which needs a different assessment, because creating a crease alone will not lift a weak lid.

Patients who form keloid scars, take blood thinners, or have had previous eyelid surgery should mention this before travel, as it affects technique and healing. Those seeking a dramatically Westernized eye that does not match their features are usually counseled toward a result that suits their face. A careful consultation, with photos reviewed in advance, confirms which method fits your anatomy and whether any add-on such as epicanthoplasty is appropriate for you.
sections.procedure_steps:
Care starts with a consultation, often begun remotely with photographs and completed in person. The surgeon examines your lid thickness, skin, fat, and natural symmetry, then discusses crease height and whether the non-incisional or incisional method suits you. The planned crease is marked precisely while you are sitting up, because lid position changes when lying down.

The procedure is performed under local anesthesia, usually with light sedation, so you remain comfortable and can sometimes open your eyes briefly to check symmetry. In the non-incisional method, the surgeon makes several tiny punctures and passes fine sutures to fold and anchor the crease, leaving no long external scar. In the incisional method, a fine cut is made along the marked line, small amounts of excess skin, muscle, or fat are removed as needed, and the crease is secured with sutures, producing a more defined and durable fold.

If agreed in advance, an epicanthoplasty is performed at the inner corner to lengthen the eye's appearance, using a small, carefully placed incision. Where a drooping lid is confirmed, ptosis correction adjusts the lid-lifting muscle, which is a distinct step from crease creation.

The operation typically takes around one to two hours depending on method and whether add-ons are included. Fine sutures are placed, cool compresses are started, and you are given detailed aftercare instructions. Most patients go home the same day rather than staying overnight.
sections.recovery_timeline:
Swelling and mild bruising around the eyes are expected and peak in the first two to three days. Cool compresses, keeping the head elevated, and resting the eyes help settle this. Discomfort is usually mild, described as tightness or a foreign-body sensation rather than significant pain, and is managed with simple prescribed medication.

For the incisional method, external sutures are typically removed around day 4 to 7 at a follow-up visit, which fits the roughly 5-day stay; the non-incisional method has no long suture line to remove. Most international patients are cleared to fly home toward the end of this window once the surgeon confirms early healing is on track. At departure the eyes still look swollen, and the crease appears higher and tighter than it eventually will.

Over the first two to three weeks, the bulk of the swelling subsides and the crease begins to soften toward a natural height. Many patients return to desk work within several days, using glasses or rest as needed, while avoiding strenuous activity, rubbing the eyes, and eye makeup until cleared.

The crease continues to settle and look more natural over the following two to three months as residual swelling resolves and any incision line fades. KmedTour coordinators arrange your suture-removal visit before departure and relay later questions to the clinic, so your healing is supported after you return home.
sections.cost_breakdown:
The stated range of $1,500 to $4,000 reflects the difference between a straightforward non-incisional crease and a more involved incisional case, or one that adds epicanthoplasty or ptosis correction. The quote usually covers the surgeon's fee, local anesthesia and sedation, the facility, standard pre-operative checks, and follow-up visits during your stay.

It generally excludes flights, accommodation, optional add-ons you decide to include, and any unrelated treatments. Add-ons such as epicanthoplasty or ptosis correction change both the price and the recovery, so confirm in writing exactly what your personal quote covers before you travel.

Korea is competitive on price largely because of the sheer volume of eyelid surgery performed, which supports efficient, experienced clinics, and because facility and staffing costs differ from those in the United States and parts of Europe. For international patients the goal is accredited, experienced care at a total cost, including travel, that often compares favorably with equivalent work closer to home. KmedTour provides an itemized quote so you compare the same scope rather than headline figures alone.
sections.why_korea:
Double eyelid surgery is among the most commonly performed cosmetic procedures in Korea, so surgeons accumulate deep experience in matching crease height and method to each patient's anatomy, including the heavier lids common among patients from Africa, the Middle East, and Asia who have no natural crease. This volume supports refined, conservative techniques aimed at natural results.

Korean clinics are typically well equipped, with established protocols for anesthesia, sterilization, and aftercare. The medical tourism sector is supported by the Ministry of Health and Welfare and promoted through KHIDI's Medical Korea program, and accreditation frameworks such as KOIHA exist to recognize facilities meeting defined standards for international patient care.

For patients traveling from abroad, this combination of focused surgical experience, modern facilities, and an established system for hosting international patients, including interpretation and coordinated scheduling, is what makes the trip practical. KmedTour connects you with accredited hospitals and helps verify a facility's credentials so your choice rests on real standards rather than marketing.

faqs.q: Is a 5-day stay enough for double eyelid surgery?
faqs.a: Yes, for most patients. The roughly 5-day stay covers consultation, surgery, and early healing, with suture removal around day 4 to 7 for the incisional method. The surgeon usually clears you to fly toward the end of that window. Your eyes will still be swollen at departure, and the crease keeps softening to its natural look over the following weeks.

faqs.q: When can I fly home after eyelid surgery?
faqs.a: Most patients are cleared to fly toward the end of the 5-day stay, once the surgeon confirms early healing and, for the incisional method, removes the sutures. Flying with swollen, recently operated eyes is manageable, but you should follow aftercare instructions on the plane, such as keeping the eyes rested and avoiding rubbing. Your coordinator schedules the check before departure.

faqs.q: Will I have language support during treatment?
faqs.a: Yes. KmedTour works with hospitals that provide interpretation for international patients, and coordinators assist with appointments, consent forms, and aftercare instructions in a language you understand. Let us know your preferred language in advance so the clinic can arrange an interpreter for your consultation, the marking of the crease, and your follow-up visits.

faqs.q: What if the creases look uneven or I want a revision later?
faqs.a: Mild early asymmetry is common while swelling settles unevenly, and it often evens out over the first weeks to months. Revision, if genuinely needed, is usually considered only after the crease has fully settled. KmedTour relays your concerns to the treating clinic and helps coordinate any follow-up discussion, so you are not handling it alone after returning home.

Most patients report mild discomfort rather than real pain, often a feeling of tightness, dryness, or something in the eye, controlled with simple prescribed medication. The procedure is done under local anesthesia with sedation, so you do not feel it during surgery. The first few days, when swelling peaks, are the most uncomfortable, easing steadily as the eyes settle.
